dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a call type of fall with a reported back injury.
Hi, I'm Sam, an EMT with the crew on scene. Can you tell me what happened today?
I don't remember falling, but I have pain in my lower back. It started this morning.
You're alert and oriented-eye opening is spontaneous, you respond appropriately to questions, and your motor response is normal.
Airway is clear and patent, and you're self-maintaining it.
You're breathing rapidly and shallowly-tachypnea. Assessment shows decreased air movement with mild rhonchi bilaterally.
Skin is warm and dry, peripheral pulses are present, and there is no cyanosis noted.
Pulse is 111-tachycardic.
Respiratory rate is 24-tachypnea is present.
Blood pressure is 176 over 78-elevated and hypertensive.
SpO2 is 96-within normal limits for ambient room air.
Glucose is 160-normal range.
EKG is sinus tachycardia without ST changes.
Eye opening is spontaneous-4 points on AVPU.
You're oriented and following commands-verbal response is intact.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact with no rashes or lesions observed.
Trachea is midline, pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation (PERRLA).
No jugular venous distention present, and no signs of nuchal rigidity.
No nasal drainage or oral secretions are obstructive at this time.
Chest is symmetric with no rib deformity; breath sounds are decreased with mild rhonchi noted bilaterally.
Abdomen is soft, nontender, with no signs of rigidity or guarding.
Pelvis is stable with no tenderness on palpation.
All four extremities show positive motor and sensory response (PMSx4), with no deformities or angulation.
What medications are you currently taking? And when was the last time you ate or drank?
You have no known drug allergies.
I take Prednisone, Phenergan, Zyrtec, and others for brain cancer, hypertension, and allergies.
You have a history of brain cancer, uncontrolled hypertension, and asthma.
Your caregiver says you had breakfast around four in the morning before the fall.
According to your caregiver, you fell while standing and returned to bed.
No signs of acute neurological deficits or slurred speech at the time of assessment.
No neck pain reported and no cervical deformities or swelling evident.
Applying full spinal motion restriction per protocol 4-13-X-collar in place.
Inserting 20-gauge IV in left arm, giving 50 ml normal saline as per protocol.
Placing 4-lead EKG-still showing sinus tachycardia.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is your pain right now?
I'd say a 4.
Recent vitals: pulse has dropped slightly to 109, respiratory rate is now 32, and blood pressure is 124 over 46.
SpO2 is 95 percent and remains stable.
Glucose could not be measured using the current system.
Securing you on the backboard with full head and body restraints and immobilization with the X-collar.
Our hospital of destination has been notified-Richmond Community Hospital is prepped for arrival.
You remain on the backboard, secured with spider straps and head locks for full spinothoracic immobilization.
Stretcher is set for supine position throughout the ride-no repositioning until stabilization in ER.
Departing with vitals: 178 over 103, respiratory rate of 32, and SpO2 of 96.
No change in rhythm or neurological status-still stable with EKG showing sinus tachycardia.
Arrival preparation includes direct transition from backboard to ER bed per stretcher protocols.
Your caregiver is riding with us, seated and secured in the front captain's seat for reassurance and assistance.
No additional interventions commenced during transit.
EKG, IV insertion, and MRI numbers are documented properly for the receiving team's reference.
Final handover will be to Katelyn Schult in the emergency department-you are under their care upon arrival.
